metadata.title: Blink: The Power of Thinking Without Thinking — Book Review

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Rapid, unconscious cognition can read situations accurately from thin slices, sometimes outperforming deliberate analysis. Consciousness itself can impair this facility: overthinking a snap judgment corrupts an otherwise reliable instinct. Emotion starts on the face, so cues like contempt become the single most reliable predictor of divorce. Thin-slicing also misleads, as with the race IAT and Harding winning the presidency for merely looking presidential.

artifactLines: Rapid, unconscious cognition can read situations accurately from thin slices, sometimes outperforming deliberate analysis.
Consciousness itself can impair this facility: overthinking a snap judgment corrupts an otherwise reliable instinct.
Emotion starts on the face, so cues like contempt become the single most reliable predictor of divorce.
Thin-slicing also misleads, as with the race IAT and Harding winning the presidency for merely looking presidential.

beats.narration_text: The idea that consciousness can impair unconscious facility unsettled me, especially paired with contempt predicting divorce and Warren Harding winning on looks alone. I want to understand when to trust the snap judgment versus override it. Can you (1) lay out the mechanism by which rapid cognition reads thin slices like a face's contempt, (2) contrast a case where it excelled with the Harding error where it failed, and (3) give me a checklist for deciding when deliberate thought corrupts a good instinct?

beats.narration_text: Gladwell took the race IAT himself and found it deeply uncomfortable. Warren Harding became president because he looked presidential—6’3”, silver-haired, commanding—despite possessing perhaps the emptiest mind ever to occupy the office.
